core_behavior.note: All three models agree on the core rate-limiting behavior but none explicitly addresses consistency across distributed instances.

inputs.note: Model B specifies detailed structured inputs including HTTP method, endpoint path, and configuration constants, while Models A and C specify only authentication tokens and timestamps at a high level.

outputs.note: Model B specifies extensive additional response headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) and a structured JSON error body, while Models A and C specify only the required HTTP 429 and Retry-After header.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes a sliding window algorithm while Model B explicitly assumes a fixed window (tumbling) algorithm aligned to clock-minute boundaries, and Model C also assumes a sliding window — Models A/C and B are directly incompatible on the windowing strategy.

failure_modes.note: Model A defaults to denying requests when the rate-limit store is unavailable, Model C returns HTTP 500, and Model B does not address store unavailability as a failure mode.

edge_cases.note: Models differ on edge cases covered: Model B addresses the 200-request burst across window boundaries specific to fixed windows, Model C addresses invalid tokens and clock skew, and Model A addresses boundary-second counting and clock drift.

security_constraints.note: Model B explicitly specifies that unauthenticated requests must be rejected with 401 before rate-limit checks and must not consume quota, while Models A and C do not specify this ordering or quota-protection behavior; none explicitly addresses per-user vs per-IP identity binding as a security constraint.

performance_constraints.note: Model B has an empty performance_constraints array while Models A and C specify latency, scalability, and concurrency thresholds.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in three fields: inputs, outputs, and edge_cases.
